The Monitoring Blind Spot

The assets you can't monitor are the ones most likely to fail.

30–40% of critical rotating equipment has no condition monitoring. Submerged pumps, enclosed drives, ATEX-zone motors. They run until they break. The failures are expensive, preventable, and invisible until they happen.

Start Your Asset Audit See How ESA Works
30–40%
of critical rotating assets have no condition monitoring
Industry estimates
€170K
average cost per unplanned equipment failure in process industries
DuPont case study
70,000
manual inspection trips replaced per year at a single water utility
Yorkshire Water
£390K
potential fines avoided from a single pump failure detection
Yorkshire Water

How companies handle it today

Most industrial operators use one of three approaches to manage the assets they can't easily reach. Each has a hard ceiling.

Scheduled Maintenance

Fixed-interval servicing regardless of actual condition. Teams replace bearings, seals, and couplings on a calendar. Some assets get maintained too early. Others fail between visits.

No visibility between service intervals. Failures still happen on schedule gaps.

Portable Vibration Rounds

Technicians walk routes with handheld vibration analysers. Effective where they can reach the asset. But submerged pumps, sealed compressors, and hazardous-zone motors are off-limits.

Physical access required. Frequency is monthly at best. Faults develop between rounds.

Online Vibration Sensors

Permanently mounted accelerometers with continuous data feeds. The gold standard for accessible, high-criticality assets. But installation costs €2,000–5,000 per point, requires shutdowns, and is physically impossible on many asset types.

Too expensive and impractical for the 30–40% of assets that need it most.

The Proximity Penalty

Every monitoring method requires proximity to the machine. That's the problem.

Vibration accelerometers bolt to the bearing housing. Thermal cameras need line of sight. Ultrasound probes need physical contact. Every established approach shares one constraint: the sensor must be on or near the asset.

This works for accessible machines on a plant floor. It fails for the assets that drive the most expensive unplanned shutdowns: submerged pumps in wet wells, sealed compressors in ATEX zones, motors enclosed inside process equipment, and drives at remote sites.

The result is a structural blind spot. The assets with the highest failure consequence have the least monitoring coverage. Not because of negligence. Because of physics.

The common constraint

  • Vibration sensors need mounting on the bearing housing
  • Thermal imaging needs line of sight to the asset
  • Ultrasound needs physical contact with the surface
  • Oil analysis needs fluid extraction from the machine

All require physical access. All fail where access is impossible.

Close the gap from the electrical cabinet.

Electrical Signature Analysis reads the current and voltage waveforms at the motor control cabinet. Those waveforms carry the mechanical signature of the entire drivetrain: motor, coupling, gearbox, and driven load.

SAM4 captures those signatures continuously. AI algorithms detect developing faults. Samotics engineers validate every alert before it reaches your team. The result: confirmed maintenance recommendations weeks to months before failure. No sensors on the machine.

  • Monitor submerged, enclosed, and hazardous-zone assets for the first time
  • Install in under 30 minutes per asset. No shutdown. No scaffolding.
  • Detect bearing wear, cavitation, misalignment, and winding defects
  • Every alert validated by a Samotics reliability engineer
Learn how ESA works →
ESA signal flow diagram
MCC capture → waveform analysis → AI classification → confirmed work order

Does this apply to your plant?

If you operate any of these asset types, you likely have a monitoring blind spot. ESA closes it from the MCC.

Submerged pumps

Wet wells, lift stations, borehole pumps. No physical access to the asset. No sensor mounting point.

ATEX / hazardous zones

Sealed compressors, Zone 1/2 motors. Sensor installation requires permits, shutdowns, or is prohibited entirely.

Enclosed process equipment

Motors inside machines, integrated drives. The bearing housing is not accessible without disassembly.

Remote or unstaffed sites

Pumping stations, compressor stations, booster sites. No on-site technician for walk-around rounds.

Harsh environments

High temperature, high vibration, corrosive atmospheres. Sensors fail or deliver unreliable data.

Large motor fleets

Hundreds of motors where per-asset sensor costs make full coverage uneconomic. ESA scales at a fraction of the cost.

The economics of the blind spot

Unmonitored assets don't just fail. They fail expensively. Here's what organisations report before and after closing the gap.

Without Monitoring
Unplanned downtime per incident €50–170K
Emergency repair premium 3–5×
Regulatory fines (single event) £390K
Manual inspection trips / year 70,000+
Energy waste from undetected inefficiency 40–55%
With ESA Monitoring
Equipment downtime reduction 40%
Cost prevented over 3 years €1.7M
Average payback period 8 months
Pump efficiency improvement 7.1%
Pollution events prevented (single detection) 3
"SAM4 detected possible bearing damage to the gearbox motor, which we had not picked up through our vibration monitoring."
Schiphol Airport — fault missed by vibration, caught by ESA

SAM4 works if your site has:

Any motor-driven asset connected through a motor control cabinet is a candidate for ESA monitoring.

AC induction motors

Fixed-speed and variable-frequency drive (VFD) motors. Both supported.

5 Hz and above

Standard 50/60 Hz mains-fed and VFD output frequencies covered.

Motor running under load

Intermittent duty cycles supported with minimum run-time thresholds.

Access to MCC cabinet

Current clamps at the cabinet. No access to the machine. No shutdown needed.

Identify the assets your monitoring can't reach.

Our asset audit maps the critical rotating equipment in your plant with no condition monitoring. Takes 30 minutes. Reveals where failures are most likely.
